title: `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools

learningObjectives.text: Explain reducer pattern using a new example and justify the result.
Explain actions and transitions using a new example and justify the result.
Compare `usestate` versus `usereducer` using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools is part of the mental model learners need before making reliable implementation decisions. It belongs to the **Complex State, Routing, and Styling** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Reducer pattern** → **Actions and transitions** → **`useState` versus `useReducer`**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Reducer pattern
sections.body: A reducer is a pure function from current state and an action to next state. It centralizes related transitions.

sections.title: Actions and transitions
sections.body: Actions describe what happened; reducers decide how that event changes state. Prefer domain event names to UI implementation names.

sections.title: `useState` versus `useReducer`
sections.body: useState is simplest for independent values; useReducer helps when transitions are related, numerous, or benefit from centralized testing.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The reducer centralizes related transitions as explicit actions.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools connects Reducer pattern, Actions and transitions, `useState` versus `useReducer`.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Reducer pattern” in the context of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools?

choices.label: Ask whether logic is caused by an event, derivable during render, or synchronizing with an external system. Only the last generally needs an Effect.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Reducer pattern.

choices.label: Render props pass behavior through a function, HOCs wrap components, and compound components coordinate related children through shared context.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Reducer pattern.

choices.label: A reducer is a pure function from current state and an action to next state. It centralizes related transitions.
choices.feedback: Reducer pattern is best understood as follows: A reducer is a pure function from current state and an action to next state. It centralizes related transitions.

choices.label: Start with user-visible outcomes and convert them into explicit behavior, inputs, edge cases, and acceptance conditions. Break large capabilities into end-to-end slices that can be implemented and verified independently.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Reducer pattern.

questions.explanation: Reducer pattern is best understood as follows: A reducer is a pure function from current state and an action to next state. It centralizes related transitions.
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.

questions.prompt: A learner must use `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: Apply Reducer pattern deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: What is the most accurate explanation of the following `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools example?

choices.label: The snippet guarantees that all values are immutable and all operations are asynchronous.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: The snippet removes the need to understand the data flowing through the program.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: The snippet is valid only because JavaScript ignores variable scope and evaluation order.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: The reducer centralizes related transitions as explicit actions.
choices.feedback: The correct choice connects the example to the underlying model rather than treating the syntax as isolated trivia.

questions.explanation: The correct choice connects the example to the underlying model rather than treating the syntax as isolated trivia.
questions.hint: Trace data, control flow, ownership, or rendering step by step.

questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.”?

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: The statement is always correct, so no further reasoning about `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools is needed.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

questions.explanation: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.
questions.hint: Look for the option that corrects both the belief and the learner's next action.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools?

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Reducer pattern, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools?

choices.label: Use `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: Avoid `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

choices.label: Use `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of `useReducer` and Choosing State Tools.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
